1.description: Vote share of nationalist parties. This figure plots the election vote share of the parties we classify as nationalist, in percent. For each country, we use either the most recent national parliamentary election as of January 1, 2017 or the same country's May 2014 European Parliament election, whichever occurs later. The vote share is plotted against country-level measures of the Gini coefficient of disposable net income (Panel A), the share of income going to the top 10% of earners (Panel B), trade balance as a fraction of GDP (Panel C), and the ratio of stock market capitalization to GDP (Panel D). The circle around each country's observation has an area proportional to the country's GDP. The slope and itst-statistic are from the GDP-weighted cross-country regression.

2.description: Vote share of anti-immigrant parties. This figure plots the election vote share of the parties we classify as anti-immigrant, in percent. For each country, we use either the most recent national parliamentary election as of January 1, 2017 or the same country's May 2014 European Parliament election, whichever occurs later. The vote share is plotted against country-level measures of the Gini coefficient of disposable net income (Panel A), the share of income going to the top 10% of earners (Panel B), trade balance as a fraction of GDP (Panel C), and the ratio of stock market capitalization to GDP (Panel D). The circle around each country's observation has an area proportional to the country's GDP. The slope and itst-statistic are from the GDP-weighted cross-country regression.

3.description: Vote share of anti-elite parties. This figure plots the election vote share of the parties we classify as anti-elite, in percent. For each country, we use either the most recent national parliamentary election as of January 1, 2017 or the same country's May 2014 European Parliament election, whichever occurs later. The vote share is plotted against country-level measures of the Gini coefficient of disposable net income (Panel A), the share of income going to the top 10% of earners (Panel B), trade balance as a fraction of GDP (Panel C), and the ratio of stock market capitalization to GDP (Panel D). The circle around each country's observation has an area proportional to the country's GDP. The slope and itst-statistic are from the GDP-weighted cross-country regression.

4.description: Support for protectionism. This figure plots the extent to which the country's respondents in the 2013 ISSP survey agree with the statement “Country should limit the import of foreign products.” The survey responses range from 1 to 5, with 5 indicating “agree strongly” and 1 “disagree strongly,” so that a higher score indicates stronger support for protectionism. The country-level score is the average of all individual responses in the country. This score is plotted against country-level measures of the Gini coefficient of disposable net income (Panel A), the share of income going to the top 10% of earners (Panel B), trade balance as a fraction of GDP (Panel C), and the ratio of stock market capitalization to GDP (Panel D). The circle around each country's observation has an area proportional to the country's GDP. The slope and itst-statistic are from the GDP-weighted cross-country regression.

5.description: Inequality and skewness of the consumption distribution. This figure plotsVtkandStkas a function ofδtfork∈{US,RoW}, under globalization (solid line) and autarky (dashed line). [Colour figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

6.description: Top shares and their ratios: Data versus model. Panel A plots the annual time series of the top 1% income share in the United States. Panel B plots the time series of top income share ratios, namely, top 1% to top 10% (solid line) and top 0.1% to top 1% (dashed line), in the United States. Both series are based on income net of taxes and transfers from the World Inequality Database. Panels C and D plot analogous quantities for equilibrium consumption shares generated under the expected path from our model (i.e., settingdZt=0in equation (5)). [Colour figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

7.description: Top wealth shares and their ratios: Data versus model. Panel A plots the annual time series of the top 1% wealth share in the United States. Panel B plots the time series of top wealth share ratios, namely, top 1% to top 10% (solid line) and top 0.1% to top 1% (dashed line), in the United States. Both series are based on net personal wealth from the World Inequality Database. Panels C and D plot analogous quantities for equilibrium wealth shares generated under the expected path from our model (i.e., settingdZt=0in equation (5)). [Colour figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

8.description: Trade deficit. This figure plots the trade deficits of the two countries, as a percentage share of local GDP, againstδt. [Colour figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

9.description: The populist vote share. This figure plots the fraction of U.S. agents voting for the populist candidate, in percent. The vertical line denotesδ¯from Proposition5. [Colour figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

10.description: Characteristics of populist and mainstream voters. This figure plots the distributions of risk aversionγi(Panel A), inequality aversionηi(Panel B), and wealth at the time of the election (Panel C) across populist voters (solid line) as well as mainstream voters (dashed line). [Colour figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

11.description: The distribution of consumption. This figure plots the distribution of consumption at timeτacross populist voters (Panel A) and mainstream voters (Panel B) under two regimes: globalization (solid line) and autarky (dashed line). The value ofδτis such that half of U.S. agents favor each regime. [Colour figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

12.description: The effects of redistribution. This figure plots the correlation between risk aversionγiand initial endowmentwiacross U.S. agents (Panel A) and the thresholdδ¯from Proposition5(Panel B) for different values of the redistribution coefficienty. [Colour figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

13.description: Asset pricing implications. This figure plots asset pricing quantities as a function ofδtat timet=τ−2years. Panel A plots the market prices of risk,σπUSandσπRoW, under globalization (solid line) and under autarky (dashed and dash-dot lines, respectively). Panel B plots the global market share of U.S. stocks,PtUS/(PtUS+PtRoW). Panel C plots the yields on U.S. and RoW 10-year zero-coupon bonds. The vertical line in Panels B and C denotes the thresholdδ¯from Proposition5. [Colour figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

14.description: Portfolio holdings and expected returns. Panel A plots model-implied distributions of the proportion of wealth invested in equity, for both U.S. agents and RoW agents. Panel B plots equity positions adjusted for leverage and taxes, along with the corresponding positions from the data, both for U.S. agents. The latter positions correspond to Figure8in Guiso and Sodini (2013), except that we plot percentiles rather than deciles. We thank Paolo Sodini for sharing the percentile data. Panel C plots model-implied expected portfolio returns. In all three panels, we condition onδt=1, and all quantities are plotted as functions of agents' net worth, expressed in percentiles. [Colour figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]
